name: Fort Bourtange
shortDescription: A perfectly preserved five-pointed star fort now serving as an open-air museum.
description: Built in 1593 by William I of Orange to control the only road between Germany and the city of Groningen, Fort Bourtange is one of the best-preserved star forts in the world. Its five-pointed bastion design with moats, ramparts, and earth walls created interlocking fields of fire that eliminated blind spots. Converted into a village and later a museum, visitors can still walk its ramparts and moats.

name: Palmanova
shortDescription: A Renaissance ideal city built as a perfect nine-pointed star fortress.
description: Founded by the Republic of Venice in 1593, Palmanova is a textbook example of Renaissance military urbanism — a fortified city laid out as a perfectly symmetrical nine-pointed star. Every street radiates from a central hexagonal piazza, and three concentric lines of fortification surrounded the settlement. Napoleon later added an outer ring of defences. It remains one of the world's best-preserved Renaissance fortified towns.

name: Kronborg Castle
shortDescription: Shakespeare's 'Elsinore' — a Renaissance fortress controlling the sound between Denmark and Sweden.
description: Commanding the narrow strait between Denmark and Sweden, Kronborg collected tolls from every ship passing through for three centuries. Built in the Renaissance style with bastioned outer works, it represents some of the finest military architecture in Northern Europe. Shakespeare used it as the setting for Hamlet, calling it 'Elsinore.' Its casemates once housed 1,000 soldiers and 56 cannons.

name: Fort Saint Elmo
shortDescription: The star fort that held out against the Ottoman army during the Great Siege of Malta.
description: Located at the tip of the Sciberras Peninsula in Valletta, Fort Saint Elmo played a crucial role in the Great Siege of Malta in 1565. Outnumbered Knights Hospitaller held the fort for 31 days against a massive Ottoman force, inflicting devastating casualties before it finally fell. The fort's sacrifice bought time and helped save Christian Europe. It was subsequently rebuilt stronger as a star fort.
heritageStatus: UNESCO World Heritage Site (part of Valletta)

name: Fort Jefferson
shortDescription: A massive hexagonal brick fort on a coral island in the Gulf of Mexico.
description: Built on Garden Key in the Dry Tortugas, Fort Jefferson is one of the largest masonry fortifications ever constructed in the Americas. Its hexagonal walls — up to 50 feet high and 8 feet thick — enclose a 16-acre interior. Construction lasted from 1846 to 1875 but was never fully completed. The fort is famous for housing Dr. Samuel Mudd, who was imprisoned for treating John Wilkes Booth's leg.

name: Fort Ticonderoga
shortDescription: The 'Key to a Continent' — a pivotal French and Indian War star fort.
description: Built by the French in 1755 and originally named Fort Carillon, Fort Ticonderoga controlled the strategic waterway connecting Lake Champlain and Lake George in upstate New York. In 1758 it was held by 3,500 French troops who repelled a force of 15,000 British and colonial soldiers. Captured by Ethan Allen and his Green Mountain Boys in 1775, its cannons were used to force the British from Boston.

name: Naarden Vesting
shortDescription: Europe's best-preserved double-moated star fortress, with six bastions.
description: Naarden is a remarkable example of a late 17th-century Vauban-style star fort with six bastions, a double moat, and concentric outworks. The town was razed and its fortifications rebuilt after a Spanish massacre in 1572 to serve as a key defensive link in the 'Waterlinie' — Holland's defensive water line. Today it is one of the best-preserved fortified towns in the world.

name: Castillo San Felipe de Barajas
shortDescription: The largest Spanish colonial fort in the Americas, guarding Cartagena de Indias.
description: Perched on a hill overlooking Cartagena, Castillo San Felipe de Barajas is the largest and most formidable fortress built by the Spanish in their American colonies. Its ingenious design — featuring a network of tunnels that allowed communication and supply movement between sections — allowed a small garrison to control the entire structure. The British Admiral Vernon failed to take it in the War of Jenkins' Ear despite overwhelming numbers.

name: Neuf-Brisach
shortDescription: Vauban's masterpiece — an octagonal fortified town built from scratch.
description: Designed by Sébastien Le Prestre de Vauban, Louis XIV's military engineer, Neuf-Brisach was built from scratch after France ceded Breisach to the Habsburgs in 1697. Its perfectly octagonal plan with eight bastions, ditches, and counterguards is a landmark of military architecture. Vauban considered it his finest work, and Louis XIV called it 'the most beautiful fortification in the world.'
